original_text: [I]n our adversary system of criminal justice, any person haled into court, who is too poor to hire a lawyer, cannot be assured a fair trial unless counsel is provided for him.
modified_text: In Gideon v. Wainwright, 372 U.S. 335 (1963), the Supreme Court held that the Sixth Amendment right to counsel is a fundamental right made obligatory on the states by the Fourteenth Amendment, so that an indigent defendant in a state criminal prosecution must be provided with appointed counsel.
claim: Gideon held the Sixth Amendment right to counsel is obligatory on the states via the Fourteenth Amendment, requiring appointed counsel for indigent defendants in state criminal prosecutions.
notes: Faithful and accurate statement of the actual holding. Justice Black wrote for the majority in 1963; the Court overruled Betts v. Brady and held the Sixth Amendment right to counsel fundamental and obligatory on the states through the Fourteenth Amendment, requiring appointed counsel for indigent defendants in state criminal prosecutions. Citation, author, year, and court all correct. Verified via CourtListener and Cornell LII.
